content.body:
FYI, I added the following slide to the [Amount presentation](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Wq4uT0-lx8A_rfoSQ9uy5cnH1WHZXVjRmfYuugqkjQo/edit?usp=sharing) to address waldemar's review comments:
> **Open issue: Converting 5 grams to tonnes results in 0.0000049999999999999996 tonnes**
>
> As pointed out by Waldemar Horvat, using Number multiplication for conversions can lead to surprising results:
>     5 * 1e-6 === 0.0000049999999999999996
> We are open to iterating on and improving the ConvertUnitValue algorithm during Stage 2, as long as we don't end up imposing a requirement on implementations to support arbitrary precision arithmetic, or support for rational values.
content.formatted_body:
FYI, I added the following slide to the Amount presentation to address waldemar's review comments:

Open issue: Converting 5 grams to tonnes results in 0.0000049999999999999996 tonnes

As pointed out by Waldemar Horvat, using Number multiplication for conversions can lead to surprising results:
5 * 1e-6 === 0.0000049999999999999996
We are open to iterating on and improving the ConvertUnitValue algorithm during Stage 2, as long as we don't end up imposing a requirement on implementations to support arbitrary precision arithmetic, or support for rational values.
